perturbation.original_text: Additional Work shall be defined as the addition or revision of pages, graphics, or other features, any text, graphic or page design or programming requiring more than two rounds of textual or graphical revisions, substantial revisions to text and/or content provided by Client, changes to elements which have been finalized, or significant changes in the Scope of Work.
perturbation.changed_text: Additional Work shall be defined as work that the Company deems additional, revisions of pages, graphics, or other features, any text, graphic or page design or programming edits, revisions to text and/or content provided by Client, changes to elements which have been finalized, or changes in the Scope of Work.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Additional Work' was specific and relatively objective. By changing it to include 'work that the Company deems additional,' the definition becomes highly subjective and gives the Company broad discretion to charge extra for tasks that might have been included in the original Contract Price. This could be seen as an unfair or deceptive practice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices (UDAP) laws
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts UDAP laws because it creates a situation where the Company can unilaterally decide what constitutes 'Additional Work' and charge extra for it, potentially without clear justification or prior agreement. This lack of transparency and potential for arbitrary charges could be deemed an unfair or deceptive practice.

Español Report Fraud Get Consumer Alerts Search the Legal Library Federal Trade Commission Act Tags: Consumer Protection Competition Appliances Alcohol Automobiles Clothing and Textiles Finance Franchises, Business Opportunities, and Investments Funerals Jewelry Real Estate and Mortgages Tobacco Advertising and Marketing Children Endorsements, Influencers, and Reviews Environmental Marketing Health Claims Made in USA Online Advertising and Marketing Telemarketing Advertising and Marketing Basics Credit and Finance Credit and Loans Debt Debt Collection Mortgages Payments and Billing Privacy and Security Children's Privacy Consumer Privacy Credit Reporting Data Security Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Red Flags Rule Mission Competition Consumer Protection Law 15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58, as amended Links https://www.ftc.govhttp://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml Files File Safe Web Act (194.69 KB) The Federal Trade Commission Act is the primary statute of the Commission. Under this Act, as amended, the Commission is empowered, among other things, to (a) prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce; (b) seek monetary redress and other relief for conduct injurious to consumers; (c) prescribe rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive, and establishing requirements designed to prevent such acts or practices; (d) gather and compile information and conduct investigations relating to the organization, business, practices, and management of entities engaged in commerce; and (e) make reports and legislative recommendations to Congress and the public. A number of other statutes listed here are enforced under the FTC Act. Return to top

Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission (a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition i
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts UDAP laws, specifically regarding unfair or deceptive practices. The original text defined 'Additional Work' with some objectivity, while the changed text gives the Company broad discretion to deem work as 'additional' and charge extra, potentially without clear justification. This violates the principle of preventing unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce as outlined in the Federal Trade Commission Act, detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1: '(a) prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce'. The change introduces an element of arbitrariness, allowing the Company to unilaterally increase costs, which could be viewed as an unfair practice, aligning with 15 USC 45 regarding unlawful competition.

perturbation.original_text: Company representatives are available during Company's normal business hours which are 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. UTC +2, Monday through Friday.
perturbation.changed_text: Company representatives are available during Company's normal business hours which are 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. Local Time, Monday through Friday.
perturbation.explanation: The contract initially specifies UTC +2 as the time zone for business hours. Changing this to 'Local Time' introduces ambiguity and potential conflict, as 'Local Time' is not legally defined and could be interpreted in multiple ways (e.g., the client's local time, the company's local time, etc.).
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Requirement of Definiteness
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the requirement of definiteness in contract law. Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 33 states that a contract must be reasonably certain and definite in its terms. By changing the time zone to 'Local Time' without specifying whose local time, the contract becomes indefinite and potentially unenforceable with respect to the agreed-upon business hours.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The change from 'UTC +2' to 'Local Time' introduces ambiguity, potentially contradicting the definiteness required in contract law. The Law Explanation references Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 33, stating that a contract must be reasonably certain and definite. 'Local Time' lacks a clear definition within the contract, leading to potential disputes about which time zone applies, making it difficult to enforce the agreement. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the Law Explanation combined with the Perturbation Explanation indicates a significant contradiction with general contract law principles requiring definiteness.

perturbation.original_text: Company, either directly or through its authorized service provider, shall provide customer service (the "Customer Service") relating to Client Website consisting of replying to customer questions or complaints regarding website hosting services during the normal business hours set forth. Company is not obligated to provide any Customer Service except as specified in this Section.
perturbation.changed_text: Company, as it deems necessary, shall provide customer service (the "Customer Service") relating to Client Website consisting of replying to customer questions or complaints regarding website hosting services as possible. Company is not obligated to provide any Customer Service.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause outlined a basic obligation for customer service. Modifying it to 'Company, as it deems necessary' and 'as possible' transforms the obligation into a discretionary one. This could be interpreted as a failure to provide reasonably adequate customer service, which might violate consumer protection laws, especially where hosting services are concerned.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification potentially violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which is present in most US jurisdictions. UCC § 1-304 mandates that every contract or duty within the UCC imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. By making customer service entirely discretionary, the Company can effectively avoid any responsibility to address customer concerns, undermining the value of the hosting services purchased by the Client.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a legal contradiction because it significantly weakens the obligation for customer service, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original text mandates customer service, while the changed text makes it discretionary ('as it deems necessary' and 'as possible'). This contradicts UCC § 1-304, which imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance of every contract. By making customer service discretionary, the company can avoid addressing customer concerns, undermining the value of the hosting services. The changed language deviates from legally recognized definitions creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.
